A riparian specialist that colonizes streambanks, sandbars, and floodplains, making it essential for erosion control along Alberta waterways. Its dense root system stabilizes soils under freeze-thaw cycles common in Zone 3-4 and provides critical early-season catkins for native pollinators. Indigenous peoples used flexible stems for basket weaving and bark for medicinal preparations; classified as a specialist species by ANPC requiring appropriate riparian planting sites.

An ANPC-listed specialist native to open forests, forest edges, and dry meadows across Alberta's boreal and parkland zones, requiring careful sourcing of locally adapted ecotypes. Its bright yellow composite flowers provide late-season nectar for native bees and butterflies in Zone 3-4 landscapes. Not to be confused with invasive hawkweed species; this native species is non-aggressive and plays a role as a dynamic accumulator in sandy or disturbed soils.

A characteristic sedge of bogs, fens, and muskeg across Alberta, identified by its distinctive white cottony seed heads; requires strongly acidic, waterlogged, nutrient-poor sphagnum conditions that are difficult to replicate outside specialist bog restorations. Indigenous peoples of the North used the silky seed hairs as wicking for oil lamps and for insulation, and young shoots were occasionally eaten. Not suited to general permaculture systems but invaluable for peatland restoration and carbon sequestration projects.

A characteristic grass of Alberta's mixed-grass and dry fescue prairies, well-adapted to xeric Zone 3-4 conditions with exceptional drought tolerance once established. The distinctive long-awned seeds are dispersal-adapted but can pose a health risk to livestock and pets as awns can penetrate skin and mucous membranes. Listed by ALCLA as an important native grass for dryland restoration and available through BrettYoung seed sources; excellent for prairie-style ornamental and ecological plantings.

A mat-forming aquatic sedge-like rush native to shallow lakes, pond margins, and slow streams across Alberta; spreads via slender rhizomes to stabilize soft substrates and reduce shoreline erosion. Provides dense microhabitat for invertebrates and waterfowl foraging in Zone 3-4 wetland systems. Excellent for constructed wetland filters and naturalized pond edges; no known toxicity.
Beautiful drooping flower heads. Entire plant edible with mild onion flavour. Excellent pollinator.
Clusters of tiny white flowers. Square stems. Traditional dye plant. Good ground-layer pollinator.
Strong-flavoured black currant. High vitamin C. Traditional medicine for sore throat. Prefers moist areas.

Small native biennial gentian (sometimes called Felwort) of moist meadows, fens, and open calcareous ground, with opposite lance-shaped leaves and clusters of small upright tubular flowers that are pale purple to pinkish with a characteristic fringe of hairs at the throat. Intensely bitter throughout — the genus name reflects the traditional use of gentians as digestive bitters, though dosage is not well established. Gentiana affinis (prairie gentian) is a separate, showier blue-flowered perennial also native to Alberta.

A prairie legume listed by ALCLA that fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis, making it a valuable soil-building pioneer in dry Zone 3-4 Alberta landscapes. Roots were a historically significant food source for Indigenous peoples including the Cree and Blackfoot, eaten raw or cooked; caution is warranted as it can be confused with toxic water hemlock in early growth stages. Provides excellent nectar for native bumblebees and supports habitat for ground-nesting birds through its low, dense foliage.

A sod-forming native grass of Alberta's Eastern Slopes and dry prairies, widely used for dryland revegetation and erosion control in Zone 3-4 due to its deep rhizomatous root system and exceptional cold and drought hardiness. Available through BrettYoung and Eastern Slopes seed programs, it establishes relatively quickly for a native grass and outcompetes some introduced weeds once established. Provides critical overwintering cover and seed forage for grassland birds and small mammals in prairie permaculture systems.

Tall native rhizomatous sunflower of wet meadows, sloughs, and ditch margins, with narrow lance-shaped leaves and showy bright yellow composite flower heads up to 8 cm across in late summer. Some populations form small tubers similar to Jerusalem artichoke (H. tuberosus). Spreads aggressively by rhizomes in rich moist soils — excellent for large naturalized plantings and wildlife habitat but can overwhelm small beds.

NOTE: DUPLICATE ENTRY — 'Old Man's Whiskers' and 'Prairie Smoke' are both common names for Geum triflorum; recommend consolidating with the existing Prairie Smoke record in the database. Classic native prairie perennial with ferny pinnate leaves, nodding urn-shaped pinkish-red flowers in threes, and spectacular feathery plume-like fruiting heads that give the plant its common names. Exceptionally drought-tolerant, long-lived, and evergreen-ish (often holds leaves through winter) — excellent matrix plant for dry native meadows and rock gardens.

One of Alberta's most prized edible native plants, the young fiddleheads are a well-known spring vegetable harvested in May and must be cooked thoroughly as raw consumption has been linked to gastrointestinal illness. Thrives in the moist, shaded understory of Zone 3-4 riparian and boreal forest food forest systems, spreading via stolons to form dense colonies that suppress weeds and stabilize streambanks. Widely available at Alberta nurseries and highly recommended for shaded rain gardens, food forests, and moist native plantings; of cultural significance to numerous Indigenous nations across Canada.

Native prairie and foothills perennial resembling a blue-green dandelion, with a basal rosette of lance-shaped slightly glaucous leaves and solitary pale yellow composite flower heads on leafless stalks, followed by large dandelion-like seed heads. The milky latex was traditionally chewed as a gum by Indigenous peoples. Drought-tolerant and well-suited to dry native meadow plantings; distinguishable from true dandelion (Taraxacum) by its single unbranched flower stalk with no scales.

A rhizomatous, colony-forming herb of moist riparian and boreal edges; its enormous palmate leaves provide dense groundcover suppressing weeds along streambanks. Indigenous peoples across western Canada used leaves medicinally as wound poultices and consumed young shoots and flower stalks as spring greens. Spreads aggressively via rhizomes in wet soils; site carefully to prevent unwanted colonization.
Sap tapped like maple for syrup (lower sugar content). Bark for crafts. Beautiful white bark.

native_region: Foothills and montane grasslands, western Canada and northwestern USA
notes: A native bunchgrass of dry to mesic foothills grasslands and open ponderosa pine parklands in Alberta, valued for stabilizing rocky or well-drained soils prone to erosion. It provides critical nesting and foraging structure for ground-nesting birds and native grassland insects. Well adapted to periodic drought and low-fertility soils, making it an excellent low-maintenance component of dry permaculture polycultures.

native_region: Prairie and foothills, western Alberta and adjacent Rocky Mountains
notes: A showy, short-lived biennial or monocarpic perennial daisy of dry, well-drained prairie and rocky foothills slopes in Alberta, producing large lavender to pink flower heads relative to its compact rosette form. It is an important early-season nectar source for native bees and other pollinators in open grassland ecosystems. Self-seeds reliably in gravelly, low-fertility soils and is best established in rock gardens or dry prairie margins.

Native perennial of roadsides, clearings, and dry open ground, with woolly white-felted lance-shaped leaves and flat-topped clusters of button-like flower heads with papery pearl-white bracts surrounding small yellow centres. Dioecious (separate male and female plants) and excellent larval host for painted lady and American lady butterflies. Flowers dry beautifully and hold colour for years — a classic everlasting for cut-flower and dried arrangements.

Widespread native short-lived perennial or biennial of moist meadows, woodland edges, and disturbed ground, with soft clasping stem leaves and numerous daisy-like flower heads featuring 100+ very fine pale pink to white ray flowers around a yellow disk. One of the earliest asters to bloom — a valuable spring pollinator resource when few other natives are flowering. Self-seeds readily on open soil and behaves as a welcome pioneer in new plantings.
Fast-growing pioneer species. Small tart fruit good for jelly. Short-lived (30 yrs) but excellent nurse tree.

native_region: Boreal and montane forests, across Canada and northern USA
notes: A delicate, glaucous biennial of disturbed woodlands, rocky outcrops, and post-fire forest edges; it is a specialist pioneer species that colonizes thin, well-drained mineral soils after disturbance. The pink-and-yellow tubular flowers attract long-tongued bumblebees and are an important nectar resource in shaded woodland permaculture systems. All parts are considered toxic and should not be consumed; it is not reported as invasive and often disappears once canopy closes.

native_region: Montane stream margins, western Alberta and British Columbia to California
notes: A striking, showy perennial of mountain streambanks, seeps, and wet meadows, bearing large magenta-pink flowers that are a favoured nectar source for bumblebees and hummingbirds. It thrives in consistently moist to wet soils and is well suited to bioswale edges, rain gardens, and riparian restoration plantings in Zone 3-4 permaculture designs. Named in honour of Meriwether Lewis, it is a valuable ornamental and pollinator plant for high-moisture niches.

Native mat-forming perennial of dry open grasslands, foothills, and gravelly slopes, with silvery-woolly spatulate basal leaves and short stems bearing tight clusters of papery rose-pink flower heads resembling a cat's paw. Spreads by stolons to form dense drought-tolerant groundcover and is an important larval host for American lady butterflies. Requires sharp drainage — rots in wet or rich soils.

Native evergreen woodland perennial (also called Pink Wintergreen) of moist coniferous and mixed boreal forests, with a rosette of round glossy leathery basal leaves and upright racemes of nodding pink bell-shaped flowers. Partially mycoheterotrophic — depends on soil mycorrhizal networks for establishment, so transplanting from wild populations rarely succeeds; source only from nurseries that grow from seed in appropriate fungal substrate. Slow to establish but elegant under spruce and pine.

native_region: Moist riparian zones, western Alberta and British Columbia to Pacific Northwest
notes: A thicket-forming deciduous shrub of moist to wet riparian edges, fens, and lakeshores, producing dense panicles of deep pink flowers highly attractive to native bees, butterflies, and other pollinators throughout summer. Its suckering growth habit makes it excellent for streambank stabilization and living hedgerows, though it can spread aggressively by root sprouts in rich, moist soils and should be managed in smaller gardens. Provides dense nesting cover for songbirds and overwintering habitat for beneficial insects.

NOTE: 'Pink Spirea' is ambiguous. Neither Spiraea douglasii (Pacific coast/BC species) nor S. tomentosa (eastern steeplebush) is native to Alberta. The closest Alberta native is Spiraea alba var. latifolia (narrow-leaf meadowsweet), which has pale pink to white conical flower clusters in moist meadows and ditch margins. If the user intended one of the pink-flowered Pacific/eastern species, they are not truly Alberta-native — flag for review. Excellent late-summer pollinator shrub for rain gardens and wet borders.

A specialist dry-prairie bunchgrass native to Alberta's mixed-grass prairies, providing critical nesting cover for grassland birds and small mammals. Highly drought-tolerant with deep fibrous roots that stabilize sandy and gravelly soils. Not a primary food plant but seeds are consumed by granivorous birds; excellent low-maintenance groundcover for xeric permaculture zones.

Alberta's only native cactus, thriving on dry rocky slopes, sandy prairies, and south-facing banks in Zones 3-4; the yellow flowers are excellent pollinator resources. Pads and fruits are edible after careful spine removal and were used by Indigenous peoples as food and for wound poultices. Not invasive but can spread by detached pads; plant away from foot traffic due to barbed glochid spines which are a serious skin hazard.

A pioneer species of disturbed dry prairies, sandy flats, and roadsides throughout Alberta, with aromatic foliage that deters some herbivores and produces allelopathic compounds. Medicinally, several Indigenous groups used leaf preparations externally for skin conditions and ceremonially as a smudge plant. Not toxic at typical contact levels but internal use is not recommended due to thujone content; not invasive but self-seeds readily on open mineral soil.

A dominant bunchgrass of Alberta's mixed-grass and fescue prairies, producing distinctive long-awned seeds that self-bury by hygroscopic twisting and can penetrate animal skin, posing a hazard to livestock and pets. Deep fibrous roots reaching over a metre provide outstanding soil stabilization and organic matter accumulation on dry prairie sites. Seeds were historically consumed by some Indigenous peoples; the sharp awns require careful handling, especially around animals and children.

Carex hystericina is a wetland specialist of calcareous fens, sedge meadows, and shallow marsh margins in Alberta, identifiable by its distinctively spiky, porcupine-like fruiting spikes that offer excellent waterfowl food and cover. It is most appropriately used in constructed wetland, riparian restoration, and stormwater management projects where persistent soil saturation or shallow inundation can be maintained. Sourcing is limited to specialist native plant nurseries; it is not suited to typical garden or permaculture bed conditions without reliable wet substrate.

A low-growing specialist bunchgrass of dry, rocky, and nutrient-poor soils throughout Alberta, exceptionally tolerant of infertile acidic conditions where few other grasses persist. It produces cleistogamous (self-fertilizing) basal florets that ensure seed set even in harsh years, making it reliably persistent in difficult sites. Valuable as a stabilizing groundcover in xeric food-forest understories and rocky slope rehabilitation; provides fine-textured nesting material for ground-nesting birds.

One of the earliest prairie wildflowers to bloom — often appearing with crocuses before aspen leaf-out — with small tufts of rhombic (diamond-shaped) basal leaves and cheerful bright yellow glossy five-petaled flowers. Native to dry grasslands and aspen parkland throughout central Alberta. WARNING: all Ranunculus species contain protoanemonin and are toxic and irritating to skin and mucous membranes — do not ingest.

native_region: Western Canada, Great Plains, Northern USA
notes: A native dry-prairie cinquefoil available from Alberta native plant suppliers, producing cheerful yellow flowers that attract native bees and other pollinators across June and July on well-drained to rocky soils. The astringent roots and leaves have ethnobotanical medicinal applications among Plains Indigenous peoples for digestive and topical complaints, consistent with tannin-rich Potentilla chemistry. An excellent low-maintenance edging and groundcover species for xeric permaculture polycultures; not toxic but internal medicinal use should be approached conservatively.

Native upright cinquefoil (recently moved from Potentilla to Drymocallis) of dry prairies, roadsides, and open aspen parkland, with sticky-hairy stems, pinnate leaves of 7–11 sharply toothed leaflets, and dense flat-topped clusters of creamy-white to pale yellow five-petaled flowers. Unusual for a cinquefoil in being a specialist host for several small solitary bees. Drought-tolerant and long-lived in lean well-drained soils.
Distinctive columnar seedhead. Drooping yellow or maroon petals. Very drought tolerant.

A tall, vigorous rhizomatous grass of wet prairies, slough margins, and seasonally flooded lowlands in Alberta; forms dense colonies that provide exceptional erosion control on streambanks and wet swales. Its coarse biomass was historically used by Indigenous peoples for thatching and basket weaving. Can be assertively spreading via rhizomes so site placement should be managed in smaller permaculture systems; no known toxicity.

One of Alberta's earliest spring bloomers, emerging through snow to provide critical early-season pollen for native bees and queen bumblebees. All parts are toxic if ingested fresh due to protoanemonin; the plant loses toxicity when dried. Do not collect from wild populations; source only from reputable nurseries such as ALCLA or WAF.

native_region: Central and Western Canada, Central United States
notes: A warm-season bunchgrass prized for its fine arching texture, fragrant late-summer flowers, and exceptional drought and cold hardiness in Alberta's mixed-grass prairie ecosystems. Establishes slowly but forms long-lived, low-maintenance clumps that provide excellent winter structure and seed for granivorous birds. Seeds were historically ground into flour by some Indigenous Plains peoples.

A late-season bloomer of moist to mesic prairie and meadow habitats, providing deep-blue tubular flowers that are particularly important for long-tongued native bees including bumblebees preparing for winter. Gentiana roots have a long history of medicinal use in related species for digestive complaints, and some Indigenous peoples used roots from this genus similarly. Establish in consistently moist but well-drained soils; avoid dry sandy sites.

native_region: Western and Central Canada, Central United States
notes: A compact, well-behaved goldenrod native to Alberta's dry mixed-grass prairie, far less rhizomatously aggressive than Canada goldenrod and well-suited to small-scale permaculture plantings. Flowers are among the most important late-summer nectar sources for a vast diversity of native bees, wasps, and butterflies. Young leaves and flowers are edible raw or cooked, and the plant has documented Indigenous medicinal use for respiratory and urinary conditions.

NOTE: distinct species from Nodding Onion (Allium cernuum) already in the database — Prairie Onion is smaller, earlier-blooming, upright (not nodding), with whitish to pale pink flowers and a characteristic fibrous-netted bulb coat that gives the species its 'textile' name. Native to dry mixed-grass prairie and open gravelly slopes, strongly drought-tolerant. Bulbs, leaves, and flowers are edible with a mild onion-garlic flavor; harvest sparingly as populations are slow to recover. WARNING: never harvest any wild 'onion' that lacks the distinctive onion smell — death camas (Zigadenus) grows in the same habitats and is deadly.
Low spreading rose. Long bloom period. Smaller hips than R. acicularis but still edible.
White sage of the prairies. Ceremonial smudge plant. Silver foliage. Digestive tea. Spreads by rhizome.
Silver aromatic foliage. Traditional seasoning and smudge. Extremely drought tolerant.
Pink nodding flowers become feathery seedheads (the smoke). Early pollinator. Excellent dry garden plant.

native_region: Western and Central Canada, Northern Great Plains
notes: A native thistle of Alberta's upland prairies and meadows that is an exceptional pollinator plant, attracting bumblebees, monarch butterflies, fritillaries, skippers, and hummingbirds to its nectar-rich purple flowers. Peeled young stems, roots, and flower buds are edible and were an important food source for Plains Cree and Blackfoot peoples. Not to be confused with invasive exotic thistles; this species is native and should be distinguished carefully, though it can self-seed vigorously in open disturbed soil.

native_region: Southern Alberta, Great Plains, Central North America
notes: One of the most culturally significant edible plants of the northern Great Plains, the large starchy taproot was a dietary staple for many Indigenous nations including the Blackfoot, Cree, and Sioux, eaten raw, cooked, or dried and ground into flour. As a legume it fixes atmospheric nitrogen, making it a valuable companion in dry prairie permaculture polycultures. Avoid disturbing established plants as they are slow to re-establish; harvest sustainably and never collect from wild populations given declining native prairie habitat.

A specialist grass of moist prairies, sedge meadows, and riparian margins in Alberta; provides valuable micro-habitat structure for ground-nesting birds and small mammals. Its preference for seasonally wet or mesic soils makes it suitable for bioswales and wet prairie restoration plantings. Not known to be toxic or invasive; limited commercial availability means sourcing from ANPC-verified seed collections is recommended.
Alberta's floral emblem (shared with pasqueflower). Rosehips extremely high in vitamin C. Important pollinator.

Native wetland perennial (also called Water Avens or Indian Chocolate) of moist meadows, fens, streambanks, and ditches, with a basal rosette of pinnately divided leaves and nodding bell-shaped flowers featuring dark purple-brown calyces and pale pink-yellow petals, followed by feathery hook-tipped seed heads. Roots contain eugenol and when simmered produce a chocolate-clove-flavored beverage historically used as a cocoa substitute and astringent tonic. Tolerates wet clay and is excellent for rain gardens and pond edges.

Echinacea angustifolia is the true prairie species native to the northern Great Plains and is ecologically appropriate for Alberta grassland restoration; the Saskatchewan provenance stocked by ALCLA represents locally adapted genetics suitable for Zone 3-4 conditions. The taproot is the primary medicinal part, long used by Blackfoot, Cree, and other Plains Indigenous peoples as an anti-infective and wound treatment; harvest should be deferred until plants are at least 3-4 years old. Not toxic or invasive; prefers well-drained calcareous soils and is highly drought-tolerant once established.

native_region: Eastern North America (MN provenance; NON-LOCAL to Alberta)
notes: FLAG: Echinacea purpurea with Minnesota provenance is NOT native to Alberta; its natural range is eastern North America, making it a non-local ecotype that may not be genetically adapted to Alberta conditions and could introduce genetic contamination if wild populations of native Echinacea angustifolia are nearby. Widely stocked by ALCLA but should be used with caution in ecological restoration; better suited to edible or medicinal garden contexts away from natural areas. Strongly preferred by long-tongued bees and butterflies; roots and aerial parts have well-documented immunostimulant medicinal properties used by numerous Indigenous peoples.

A graceful woodland and forest-edge specialist grass with distinctive drooping, oat-like purple-tinged spikelets; well adapted to the dappled shade of aspen parkland and boreal transition zones common in central Alberta. It plays an important structural role as understory ground cover in food forest and woodland garden designs, suppressing weeds while providing nesting material and seed for small birds. Not toxic or invasive; classified as a specialist species with narrow habitat requirements, making site selection critical for successful establishment.

native_region: Boreal Mixedwood and Parkland, Western Canada
notes: A native woodland legume of aspen parkland and boreal forest edges in Alberta, Purple Peavine fixes atmospheric nitrogen via root nodule symbiosis, making it a valuable nitrogen-fixer in food forest understory and woodland garden designs. Its tendrilled climbing habit allows it to weave through shrub layers, and its purple flowers attract bumblebees and other long-tongued native pollinators. FLAG: Seeds and all parts of Lathyrus species contain neurotoxic compounds (lathyrogens) and are NOT considered safe for consumption; handle with awareness and do not use in edible contexts.
Purple cylindrical flower heads. Nitrogen fixer. Deep-rooted prairie plant. Tea from leaves.

A drought-tolerant bunchgrass native to dry, rocky slopes, cliff ledges, and gravelly alpine and subalpine sites across Alberta; its deep fibrous root system excels at stabilizing thin, skeletal soils prone to slippage. Provides important late-season seed forage for small mammals and ground-nesting birds in Zone 3-4 montane permaculture designs. Seed can be slow to establish and requires cold stratification; sourcing from specialist native seed suppliers is recommended.

IMPORTANT: Vicia cracca is NOT native to Alberta or North America — it is a Eurasian introduction now widespread on roadsides, field edges, and disturbed ground across Canada, and is listed as a noxious/invasive weed in some jurisdictions. Scrambling perennial legume with pinnate leaves ending in branched tendrils and showy one-sided racemes of 10–40 violet-purple pea flowers. Fixes nitrogen and is a heavy bee plant, but spreads aggressively by rhizome and seed. If an Alberta-native vetch is wanted instead, consider American Vetch (Vicia americana). WARNING: seeds contain toxic amino acids and should not be eaten.

Tall native wetland aster of moist meadows, ditches, fen edges, and streambanks, easily distinguished by its stout reddish-purple bristly-hairy stems and large open panicles of pale lavender-blue ray flowers with yellow disks. One of the latest-blooming asters, providing critical nectar for migrating butterflies and late-season bees. Tolerates heavy wet clay and seasonal flooding; excellent for rain gardens and naturalized wet borders.
Very early catkins — first major pollen source for bees. Bark contains salicin. Fast-growing riparian shrub.

native_region: Western Canada, Great Plains, Intermountain West
notes: Rabbitbrush is one of the latest-blooming native shrubs in Alberta, providing critical late-season nectar and pollen for migrating monarchs, native bees, and other pollinators when few other plants are flowering; this makes it an irreplaceable component of prairie and dry-land permaculture designs. It is highly drought-tolerant, thrives on alkaline and disturbed soils, and can serve as a pioneer and stabilizer on eroded or degraded sites. Historically, various parts were used medicinally by Indigenous peoples of the western plains for colds, chewing gum, and yellow dye from the flowers; the foliage has a strong aromatic scent and is not considered palatable or toxic in typical exposures.
Sweet flag. Wetland plant. Root chewed for sore throat and digestive aid. Culturally significant in many Indigenous traditions.

Carex raymondii is a relatively uncommon native sedge of moist to mesic open forests and meadow edges in the Canadian Rockies and foothills, included in WAF seed mixes but available in very limited commercial supply. It performs well as an understory groundcover in shaded permaculture zones with moderate soil moisture, contributing to biodiversity in native plant assemblages. Due to its restricted commercial availability, sourcing from WAF-affiliated programs or seed collection under permit from wild populations is typically required for restoration or permaculture projects.

Actaea rubra thrives in moist, shaded woodland understories and is well adapted to Alberta boreal and montane zones. All parts of the plant are toxic, particularly the bright red berries, which can cause severe gastrointestinal and cardiac symptoms in humans; it should be planted away from children and livestock. Indigenous peoples used root preparations medicinally with great caution for rheumatism and as a ceremonial plant, though handling requires care.

Aquilegia formosa is a key pollinator plant for hummingbirds and long-tongued bumblebees in western Alberta foothills and montane zones. The nectar from flowers was consumed by Indigenous peoples and the seeds and roots are toxic and must not be eaten. It self-seeds readily, spreading through woodland edges and streamside habitats, and contributes to biodiversity in food forest understory plantings.
Brilliant red bracts. Semi-parasitic on grass roots — needs host plants. Hummingbird magnet. Difficult to transplant.
Striking red winter stems. Traditional smoking-mix bark. Anti-diarrheal. Berries for wildlife. Excellent riparian plant.

native_region: Great Plains and intermountain western Canada
notes: Eriocoma richardsonii is a bunchgrass native to dry prairie and rocky slopes of Alberta, providing excellent erosion control on disturbed or thin soils. Its deep fibrous root system improves soil structure and supports native prairie ecosystem function, offering nesting and foraging habitat for grassland birds and invertebrates. The sharp-awned seeds can pose a minor hazard to grazing animals by embedding in skin and mucous membranes.

native_region: Arctic and subarctic North America and Eurasia
notes: Chamaenerion latifolium is a pioneering riparian species that colonizes gravel bars, glacial outwash, and stream banks throughout Alberta, stabilizing disturbed substrates with its spreading rhizomatous growth. Young shoots and leaves are edible raw or cooked and have been used as a nutritious vegetable by Indigenous Arctic and Subarctic peoples, with the leaves also brewed as a tea. It provides showy floral resources for native bees and is an excellent ground-stabilizing plant for permaculture riparian buffers.

A robust emergent sedge of shallow marshes and river margins, valuable for bank stabilization and riparian reclamation in Zone 3-4 Alberta. Rhizomes and pollen are edible and were used by Indigenous peoples as a starch source. Spreads vigorously by rhizome; monitor in smaller water features to prevent dominance.

Native rose-family perennial of aspen parkland edges, woodland clearings, and roadsides, with pinnately compound leaves and tall slender spikes of small bright yellow five-petaled flowers followed by distinctive bur-like fruits covered in hooked bristles that cling readily to clothing and fur. Traditionally used as an astringent herbal tea for sore throats and mild digestive complaints. Self-seeds readily via burr-dispersal — place thoughtfully if you want to avoid tracking burrs through the garden.

native_region: Great Plains and Rocky Mountain foothills, western North America
notes: Peritoma serrulata is a drought-tolerant annual of dry prairies and disturbed soils that is a premier native pollinator plant, attracting a wide diversity of bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds with its extended bloom period. Indigenous peoples of the plains boiled young leaves and seeds as a food source and produced a black pigment from the plant used in pottery decoration. As a member of Cleomaceae, it has modest nitrogen-fixing associations and is an excellent pioneer annual for disturbed or degraded Alberta prairie restoration sites.

native_region: Rocky Mountains and northern Great Plains, western Canada
notes: Festuca saximontana is a fine-leaved, low-growing bunchgrass adapted to rocky, dry, and nutrient-poor soils in Alberta's foothill and montane zones, making it ideal for low-maintenance groundcover in permaculture designs. It remains semi-green through much of the winter under snow, providing year-round soil coverage and erosion protection on slopes and disturbed areas. This grass is a key component of native prairie matrix plantings and supports grassland invertebrates and nesting birds.

A mat-forming perennial native to rocky alpine and subalpine ledges in the Rockies, well-suited to dry, well-drained scree or rock garden settings in Zone 3-4 Alberta. Its tubular lavender-pink flowers are highly attractive to native bumblebees and hummingbirds, making it a valuable pollinator plant. No significant toxicity; not a primary food or medicine species but important for dry rocky slope stabilization and ornamental value in xeric permaculture designs.

A succulent alpine perennial with thick, rose-scented rhizomes historically used by Indigenous peoples and Arctic communities as both food and adaptogenic medicine; young leaves and stems are edible raw or cooked and roots have long been used to reduce fatigue. It thrives in well-drained rocky or gravelly soils of alpine and subalpine zones, making it an excellent candidate for rock gardens and xeric food-forest understories in Alberta. Not toxic; the closely related Rhodiola rosea is a widely studied adaptogen and this species shares similar phytochemical properties.

NOTE: taxonomically close to Pink Pussytoes (Antennaria rosea) — some sources treat them as synonyms or part of the A. rosea complex; treat as a closely related parallel entry if both are kept. Low mat-forming native perennial of dry grasslands, foothills, and gravelly open ground, with silvery-woolly spatulate basal leaves and short stems bearing compact clusters of papery pink-tinged flower heads. Spreads by stolons into dense drought-tolerant groundcover and is a larval host for American lady butterflies. Requires sharp drainage; rots in wet or rich soils.
Keystone grass of northern fescue prairie. Cool season bunchgrass. Indicator of undisturbed prairie.

Elegant native woodland perennial (formerly Disporum trachycarpum) of aspen parkland and moist mixed boreal forests, with gracefully arching stems, ovate parallel-veined leaves, and pairs of hidden creamy-white bell-shaped flowers dangling beneath the foliage, followed by distinctive velvety-bumpy red-orange berries. The specific epithet trachycarpa means 'rough-fruited', referring to the textured berry surface. Berries are not considered reliably edible and reports vary — treat as ornamental only; spreads slowly by rhizome in humus-rich shaded soils.

Native alumroot of rocky outcrops, cliff ledges, and open slopes in the Rocky Mountain foothills and BC interior, with mounded rosettes of rounded deeply veined leathery basal leaves and tall narrow spikes of small cream-green bell-shaped flowers on naked stalks. Range reaches into western Alberta but it is NOT a typical Edmonton-area native — more characteristic of mountain/foothills habitats and may need extra drainage in central Alberta. Heuchera richardsonii is the more common Alberta prairie alumroot.

Tiny native carnivorous perennial of sphagnum peat bogs and fens, with a flat rosette of round long-stalked leaves covered in glistening red glandular hairs that secrete sticky mucilage to trap and digest small insects (supplementing nitrogen in nutrient-poor habitats). Produces a slender leafless stalk with small white flowers that open one at a time. Requires permanently saturated, strongly acidic, nutrient-poor substrate — essentially impossible to grow outside a dedicated bog garden. D. anglica (English sundew, with elongated leaves) also occurs in Alberta fens but is less common. Never collect from the wild.

A low-growing woodland violet native to moist coniferous and mixed forests in the Rocky Mountain foothills of Alberta, forming dense ground-covering patches suitable for shaded food-forest floor layers. Leaves and flowers are edible and nutrient-rich, with a history of use among Indigenous communities across western North America; flowers can be used in salads and leaves cooked as greens. Not toxic; provides early-season pollen for queen bumblebees and supports forest understory biodiversity in Zone 3-4 permaculture systems.

Submerged aquatic native to Alberta ponds, lakes, and slow rivers; produces starchy tubers and seeds that are critically important food for migrating waterfowl including ducks and geese, making it a keystone species in wildlife ponds. Tolerates moderate salinity and a wide range of water chemistry, including alkaline prairie sloughs. Tubers and seeds were consumed by some Indigenous peoples; rhizomatous spread can be vigorous in fertile water.

A specialist prairie wetland perennial restricted to moist saline and alkaline meadows and lake margins, making it a valuable indicator and stabilizer plant for alkaline wetland edges in Alberta's prairie and parkland regions. Its distinctive reflexed magenta petals attract specialist buzz-pollinating bees, and it is considered a species of conservation concern in parts of its range due to saline wetland habitat loss. Not toxic or invasive; best used in wetland restoration and saline-tolerant permaculture designs where few other native ornamentals thrive.

A highly salt-tolerant rhizomatous grass endemic to inland saline flats, alkali sloughs, and disturbed saline soils across the Alberta prairies; an invaluable pioneer species for reclamation of salt-affected lands in Zone 3-4. Seeds were traditionally ground into flour by some Indigenous peoples of the Great Plains, making it a rare edible grain option for saline sites. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes in suitable saline conditions; plan containment strategies in mixed plantings.

A deep-rooted, drought-adapted bunchgrass of sandy prairie dunes, stabilized sand hills, and coarse glacial outwash soils across Alberta; one of the most important species for binding and stabilizing active sand in Zone 3-4 prairie permaculture. Its extensive rhizome network builds organic matter in nutrient-poor sandy substrates, gradually improving soil quality for succession plantings. Seed requires specialist sourcing and benefits from direct-sow in autumn or cold stratification before spring planting.

native_region: Central and Western Canada, Great Plains
notes: A fast-spreading, clonal riparian shrub that colonizes sandbars, riverbanks, and disturbed floodplain sites throughout Alberta, forming dense thickets that are critical for streambank stabilization and prevention of erosion during spring flooding. Its dense structure provides essential nesting and cover habitat for numerous songbirds, waterfowl, and small mammals, while catkins are among the earliest pollen sources for native bees and overwintered butterflies. Inner bark and stems have been used by Indigenous peoples for cordage and basketry; not toxic but can spread aggressively by root sprouting in moist disturbed areas.

An early-maturing, cool-season bunchgrass native to dry open prairies and grasslands of Alberta, with a critical ecological role as one of the first grasses to green up in spring, providing early forage for wildlife and livestock before summer heat causes summer dormancy. Its seeds are palatable to ground-foraging birds including sparrows and longspurs, and it was historically harvested by Indigenous peoples across the Great Plains as a grain source. Highly drought-tolerant and non-invasive; an excellent pioneer species for dryland prairie restoration, xeric groundcover, and seed-mix components in Zone 3-4 Alberta permaculture.

A rhizomatous wetland sedge adapted to marshes, fens, and seasonally flooded meadows across Alberta's parkland and boreal zones. Spreads aggressively via rhizomes making it highly effective for riparian reclamation and stabilizing wet disturbed soils. Provides dense cover and seed for waterfowl and marsh birds; no known toxicity.
Premier prairie fruit shrub. Extremely hardy. Fruit similar to blueberry. Self-fertile but better yield with cross-pollination.

IMPORTANT: Monarda didyma is NOT native to Alberta — its native range is eastern North America (Appalachians and Great Lakes). It is a popular cultivated ornamental and widely naturalized. Wild Bergamot (M. fistulosa) is the true Alberta-native Monarda and is already in the database. Tall mint-family perennial with aromatic leaves and showy whorled clusters of tubular scarlet-red flowers highly attractive to hummingbirds, bees, and butterflies. Leaves and flowers are edible and make a classic 'Oswego tea'. Prone to powdery mildew in humid or crowded conditions.

edible_parts: flowers, young leaves, seeds (Oenothera genus; mild edibility)
notes: A drought-tolerant subshrub of dry Alberta prairies producing striking red to scarlet flowers unusual for the evening primrose family; blooms attract hawkmoths and native bees. Tolerates poor, sandy, or gravelly soils and thrives in xeric permaculture designs as a groundlayer pollinator plant. No significant toxicity; seeds and young leaves have edible potential similar to related Oenothera species used by Indigenous peoples of the plains.

native_region: Western Canada, alpine and subalpine rocky slopes
notes: A silky-hairy alpine perennial with vivid purple flower spikes and prominent stamens, native to rocky talus and scree of Alberta's mountain and foothill zones; an exceptional pollinator plant for bumble bees and solitary bees. Excellent pioneer species for disturbed, rocky, or gravelly soils in Zone 3-4 alpine permaculture designs; helps stabilize erosion-prone slopes. Contact with foliage may cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals; not considered edible.
Traditional wound herb. Low spreading pollinator. Tea for sore throat. Can function as lawn alternative.
Traditional expectorant. Root harvested for respiratory medicine. Small inconspicuous flowers. Becoming uncommon.

A fine-leaved, cool-season bunchgrass widely distributed across Alberta's prairies, foothills, and open woodlands; highly drought-tolerant and excellent for low-maintenance groundcover in Zone 3-4 permaculture systems. Forms dense tussocks that suppress weeds, prevent erosion on slopes, and provide nesting microhabitat for ground-nesting insects. Non-invasive and easy to establish from seed or plug; valued for its year-round structure and minimal care requirements.
Cyclamen-like pink flowers with reflexed petals. Moist meadows and stream edges. Spring ephemeral.

edible_parts: leaves, latex (traditional), roots (related species used similarly to dandelion)
notes: A diminutive dandelion-relative of Alberta's dry native prairies, producing small yellow composite flowers close to the ground; specialist-only availability reflects its ecological specificity to undisturbed grassland communities. All parts including leaves, roots, and latex are edible and used similarly to dandelion by Indigenous peoples of the plains, though the plant is uncommon enough that harvest from wild populations is discouraged. Best used in naturalized dry prairie permaculture plantings where it supports early-season native pollinators.

native_region: Western Canada, subalpine and alpine riparian
notes: A low-growing, mat-forming alpine and subalpine willow native to moist streambanks, fens, and gravelly riverbars throughout Alberta's mountain and boreal zones; exceptional for riparian restoration and stream-bank erosion control in Zone 2-4 permaculture designs. Catkins provide critical early-season pollen and nectar for bees before most other plants bloom, and the dense branching offers nesting cover for wetland birds. Specialist propagation reflects its ecological sensitivity; plant in consistently moist to wet soils in full sun for best establishment.
Large violet flower heads. Woodland edge species. Good late-summer colour.
Blue-purple daisy-like flowers. Long bloom. Good cut flower.

native_region: Western Canada, Rocky Mountains and foothills
notes: A stunning blue-flowered perennial native to rocky slopes, open forests, and subalpine meadows of Alberta's Rockies and foothills; widely available from native plant nurseries and ideal for shaded rock garden or food forest understory applications in Zone 3-4. The ladder-like pinnate foliage is ornamentally attractive season-long, and the vivid blue to violet flowers are highly attractive to bumble bees and butterflies. No significant toxicity; not traditionally considered edible, though related Polemonium species have occasional ethnobotanical records for medicinal use by Indigenous mountain peoples.
Dense purple flower spikes. Silvery woolly foliage. Dry grasslands. Toxic to livestock but good pollinator plant.
Monarch butterfly host plant. Large pink flower clusters. Milky sap. Critical conservation plant.

A mat-forming native herb of dry grasslands and open slopes in Alberta, Antennaria pulcherrima spreads vegetatively via stolons to form dense weed-suppressing groundcover. It provides early-season nectar for native bees and butterflies, and its fluffy seed heads offer nesting material for small birds. Some Antennaria species have been used medicinally by Indigenous peoples for respiratory ailments; this species is not considered toxic.
Yellow flowers bloom all summer. Extremely long bloom period. Very hardy and drought tolerant. Good low hedge.

Sideoats Grama is a warm-season bunchgrass native to the dry mixed-grass prairie of southeastern Alberta, highly valued for its drought tolerance and distinctive pendant spikelets that provide ornamental interest through winter. It offers important forage and cover for grassland birds and small mammals, and its deep fibrous roots stabilize soils and improve infiltration. The seeds are technically edible and were used by some Indigenous Plains peoples, though yield per plant is modest.

Silky Lupine is a showy nitrogen-fixing perennial of dry open slopes and grasslands in Alberta, with silky-hairy foliage and vibrant blue-purple flower spires that attract bumblebees and other native pollinators. Its root nodules harbour Bradyrhizobium bacteria that fix atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil fertility for companion plants in a food forest edge or pioneer planting. CAUTION: All parts of this plant, especially seeds, are toxic to humans and livestock due to quinolizidine alkaloids; it should not be used as a food plant.
Thorny. Silver leaves. Tart berries excellent after frost. Nitrogen fixer. Good hedge/windbreak.

Silver Sagebrush is a silvery-leaved aromatic shrub native to dry prairie and riparian fringes of southern Alberta, providing critical thermal and escape cover for sage-obligate birds such as Sharp-tailed Grouse and songbirds. Its deep roots access subsoil moisture and nutrients, making it a useful dynamic accumulator and soil stabilizer on eroded or disturbed sites. Various Artemisia species have extensive Indigenous medicinal use including smudging, fever treatment, and as insect repellent; direct consumption should be avoided due to high thujone content.
Wolf willow. Intensely fragrant silver flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Dry mealy fruit edible but not palatable. Excellent for reclamation.

native_region: Western North America, Rocky Mountain alpine and subalpine
notes: Oval-leaved Buckwheat is a cushion-forming specialist of rocky alpine and dry subalpine habitats in the Rocky Mountain region, with densely woolly silver leaves that reflect UV radiation and reduce water loss in exposed sites. It is a critical nectar source for specialist native bees and alpine butterflies, and its seeds are consumed by small mammals and ground-nesting birds. This species requires excellent drainage and gritty or sandy soil; it is intolerant of clay or wet conditions and performs best in rock garden or scree permaculture settings.
Spreading groundcover. Starchy roots were important Indigenous food. Silver-backed leaves.

native_region: Western Canada, Rocky Mountain foothills and montane
notes: Silvery Lupine is a drought-tolerant nitrogen-fixing perennial of montane meadows, open forests, and disturbed roadsides in Alberta, distinguished from Silky Lupine by its more silvery pubescence and variable flower colour ranging from blue to pale lavender. Like other lupines it hosts Bradyrhizobium root symbionts that enrich poor soils, making it an excellent pioneer and food-forest understory plant for establishing nitrogen capital. CAUTION: All plant parts, particularly seeds and pods, contain toxic quinolizidine alkaloids dangerous to humans, children, and grazing livestock; it must not be used as a food plant.

Sitka Valerian thrives in moist alpine and subalpine meadows of the Rocky Mountain foothills, tolerating Zone 3 winters well. The roots have a long history of Indigenous medicinal use as a sedative and nervine; the strong odour attracts pollinators and wildlife. Not invasive; prefers consistently moist, humus-rich soils in partial shade in Alberta permaculture systems.

native_region: Boreal Canada, Eastern and Western woodland
edible_parts: berries (raw or cooked, strong skunk odour when foliage crushed)
notes: Skunk Currant is a low, sprawling shrub of boreal woodland understories, well-adapted to Zone 2-3 Alberta conditions. The berries are edible and valued by wildlife including bears and songbirds, though the pungent foliage deters many browsers. Its shade tolerance and spreading habit make it useful as a forest-floor groundcover layer in permaculture food forests.

native_region: Western Canada, Alberta to British Columbia
notes: Slender Blue Beardtongue is a compact, drought-tolerant forb native to moist-to-dry grasslands and open forests in Alberta and BC, thriving in well-drained soils. Its vivid blue-purple flowers are a critical early-summer nectar source for native bees, bumblebees, and hummingbirds. Non-invasive and excellent for low-maintenance pollinator strips, rock gardens, and erosion control on sunny slopes in Zone 3-4 permaculture systems.

Native upright cinquefoil of dry to moist mixed-grass prairie, aspen parkland meadows, and foothills, with palmately compound leaves of 5–9 toothed leaflets silvery-woolly beneath, and open clusters of bright yellow five-petaled flowers on slender wiry stems. Long blooming period and drought-tolerant. Host plant for several small skipper butterflies and readily available from native-plant nurseries for meadow and pollinator plantings.

native_region: Western and Central Canada, riparian corridors
edible_parts: young leaves and shoots (cooked; stinging trichomes neutralized by heat or drying)
notes: Slender Nettle is a native riparian forb found along streambanks and disturbed woodland edges across Alberta, where it is a powerful dynamic accumulator of nitrogen, iron, and silica. CAUTION: stinging trichomes cause skin irritation on contact; handle with gloves. Young shoots are highly nutritious and widely used as cooked greens and in traditional Indigenous medicine as an anti-inflammatory and tonic; fibre from stems was historically used for cordage.
Short-lived bunchgrass. Quick establishing — good for initial restoration seedings. Self-seeds.

native_region: Wetlands and Parkland of Western Canada
notes: A native annual grass of slough margins, wet meadows, and seasonally flooded ditches across Alberta parkland; self-seeding prolifically in disturbed wetland soils, making it a reliable pioneer for wetland reclamation and riparian restoration in Zone 3-4. Seeds are eaten by waterfowl and shorebirds, providing high wildlife value, and were historically consumed by Indigenous peoples as an emergency grain. Non-invasive and easily managed; well-suited to K&S Seeds and similar native supplier stock for wetland restoration projects.

native_region: Across Canada and northern United States
notes: One of Alberta's most common emergent aquatic sedges, colonizing pond margins, fens, bogs, and slow streams from the foothills to the boreal. Its inflated perigynia are distinctive and the dense tussock-forming habit makes it excellent for bioswale and wetland restoration. Provides critical nesting and foraging habitat for waterfowl; not available in retail and must be sourced from specialist seed producers.

A specialist wetland sedge native to boreal and montane Alberta, well-suited to reclamation of degraded marsh edges and ephemeral wetlands. It provides dense cover and seed for waterfowl and shorebirds, and its rhizomatous mats stabilize soft substrates. Preferred by wetland restoration practitioners for its reliable establishment in cold, poorly drained soils.

NOTE: 'Small-leaved Everlasting' in Alberta typically refers to Antennaria parvifolia (not a variety of Pearly Everlasting / Anaphalis margaritacea, which is a taller unrelated species). Native mat-forming pussytoes of dry mixed-grass prairie with small silvery-woolly spatulate basal leaves and short stems bearing tight clusters of papery white to pale pink flower heads. Spreads slowly by stolons into low evergreen mats; excellent for dry rock gardens and as matrix groundcover. Closely related to and often confused with A. rosea and A. microphylla — consider consolidating Antennaria entries.
Blue-purple flowers. Critical late-season pollinator. Drought tolerant.
Waxy blue-green leaves. Bright blue-purple tubular flowers. Excellent for dry sunny sites. Early bloom.

Native showy fleabane of moist mixed-grass prairie, meadows, streambanks, and open woodland edges, with lance-shaped basal leaves and one to several large daisy-like flower heads per stem featuring 100+ narrow blue-violet to lavender-pink ray flowers around a yellow disk. One of the most attractive native Erigerons and one of the earlier-blooming composites on the prairie. Shorter-lived than many perennials but self-seeds modestly; excellent native-meadow pollinator plant.

native_region: Eastern and Central Canada, woodland understory
edible_parts: leaves, seeds, and roots (anise-flavoured; raw or cooked); root used medicinally
notes: Smooth Sweet Cicely is a shade-tolerant woodland herb with a distinctive anise fragrance, native to moist deciduous and mixed forest understories across Canada. All parts are edible and have been used by Indigenous peoples as a flavouring, digestive aid, and respiratory medicine; the deep taproot accumulates minerals and improves soil structure. It self-seeds readily and can naturalize into shaded food forest polycultures in Zone 3-4 Alberta, making it a valuable specialist understory plant.

native_region: Western Canada, prairie and open woodland
edible_parts: seeds (historically ground into flour by Indigenous peoples)
notes: Smooth Wild Rye is a robust, clump-forming native grass of prairie margins, open woodlands, and disturbed sites across western Canada, well-suited to Zone 3-4 Alberta conditions. Its dense root system provides excellent erosion control on slopes and streambanks, and the seed heads are an important late-season food source for songbirds and small mammals. Non-invasive and useful as a pioneer or matrix grass in native prairie restoration and permaculture windbreak understories.

Helenium autumnale is a valuable late-season pollinator plant, providing nectar for bees and butterflies in Alberta when few other natives bloom. It thrives in moist, low-lying areas and riparian margins, making it suitable for rain gardens and water-harvesting swales in Zone 3-4 systems. All parts are toxic if ingested and can cause contact dermatitis; the dried leaves were historically used as snuff by some Indigenous peoples.

Yucca glauca reaches its northern range limit in the dry Milk River and Oldman River grasslands of southern Alberta, making it a rare and ecologically significant native at the edge of Zone 3-4. It has a mutualistic relationship with the Yucca moth for pollination and provides critical structure and food in shortgrass prairie permaculture designs. Indigenous peoples used roots as soap and shampoo, wove leaves into baskets and rope, and consumed flowers and young fruits; the saponin-rich roots are mildly toxic if over-consumed.

native_region: Western Canada and Northwestern North America
notes: Arnica lonchophylla is a boreal and montane meadow specialist found across Alberta in open woodlands, forest edges, and subalpine meadows, spreading via rhizomes to form small colonies. Like other arnicas, it contains helenalin, a sesquiterpene lactone with well-documented anti-inflammatory properties used extensively in topical herbal medicine; all parts are toxic if ingested. Indigenous peoples used arnica poultices for bruises and muscle pain, and it integrates well into woodland medicine gardens in Zone 3-4 permaculture systems.

native_region: Western Canada and Rocky Mountain prairies
notes: Koeleria spicata is a cool-season bunchgrass of dry, rocky, and gravelly prairie and foothills habitats across Alberta, ecologically similar to Junegrass (Koeleria macrantha) but adapted to harsher, more exposed sites. It provides important early-season forage for wildlife and nesting cover for ground-nesting birds, and its fibrous root system excels at stabilizing disturbed dry soils in Zone 3-4 permaculture designs. It is less commonly available in native seed mixes than Junegrass but is valuable for restoring degraded shortgrass and mixed-grass prairie communities.

Submerged oxygenating plant native to Alberta ponds and slow-moving water; overwinters as dormant stem fragments under ice. Provides critical fish spawning habitat and invertebrate refuge in cold-water permaculture pond systems. Not to be confused with the invasive Eurasian water-milfoil (Myriophyllum spicatum); source only from verified native suppliers.

Native perennial of dry open woods, aspen parkland edges, roadsides, and disturbed ground, with smooth reddish stems exuding milky latex, opposite oval leaves, and loose clusters of small fragrant pink bell-shaped flowers followed by paired slender pod-like follicles that split to release silky-tufted seeds. WARNING: all parts contain cardiac glycosides and are toxic to humans, pets, and livestock — the common name 'dogbane' reflects its historical reputation for poisoning dogs. Important nectar plant for monarchs and many butterflies; stem fibers were traditionally used for cordage. Spreads by deep rhizomes and can be difficult to remove once established.

native_region: Eastern and Central Canada into northern United States
notes: A woodland sedge of moist to mesic deciduous and mixedwood forests, characteristic of shaded ravines and stream banks in Alberta's parkland and foothills regions. Its long-beaked perigynia are ecologically distinctive and it plays a role in forest understory ground layer stability. Seed must be sourced from specialist native plant suppliers; no known toxicity or invasiveness concerns.
Early spring ephemeral. Small corms taste like potato. Delicate pink-white flowers. Goes dormant by summer.

Mimulus ringens is a wetland specialist native to stream banks, marshes, and wet meadows, making it an excellent plant for constructed wetlands, bioswales, and water garden margins in Zone 3-4 Alberta permaculture systems. It is a specialist pollinator plant for long-tongued bees and provides riparian edge habitat; its presence indicates consistently wet, high-quality habitat. Leaves are reportedly edible though bitter and are used in small amounts in salads; it is at or near the western edge of its native range in Alberta.
Woodland groundcover. Star-shaped white flowers. Red-striped berries for wildlife. Shade tolerant.

Solidago glutinosa is a compact, glandular-sticky alpine and subalpine goldenrod native to rocky slopes, meadows, and open montane habitats in Alberta, offering a smaller stature than common goldenrod species and excellent suitability for Zone 3-4 rock gardens and dry permaculture polycultures. It is a critical late-season nectar source for migrating pollinators and supports specialist bees; young leaves and flowers are edible and have a long history of medicinal use among Indigenous peoples for respiratory and urinary complaints. It spreads moderately by rhizome but is not considered invasive, and its sticky leaves deter some herbivores.

Compact native goldenrod (formerly split among Solidago spathulata, S. glutinosa, and others) of rocky slopes, gravelly foothills, and dry open ground, with glossy sticky-resinous spatulate basal leaves and narrow upright spike-like clusters of small golden-yellow flower heads. Better-behaved than the tall rhizomatous prairie goldenrods — clump-forming rather than aggressively spreading. Excellent late-summer pollinator plant; like other Solidago species, leaves can be used as a mild tea. Does NOT cause hay fever (that's ragweed blooming at the same time).
Sticky glandular stems. Pink-purple flowers. Meadow and grassland species.
Flat-topped flower clusters. More compact than Canada goldenrod. Drought tolerant.

NOTE: 'Rhombic-leaved Sunflower' most likely refers to Helianthus pauciflorus (formerly H. rigidus, Stiff Sunflower), a drought-tolerant native perennial of dry mixed-grass prairie with stiff rough-textured lance-shaped leaves and showy yellow flower heads with reddish-purple disk florets. H. grosseserratus (sawtooth sunflower) is a moist-tall-grass prairie species at its western range limit and uncommon in Alberta. Spreads by rhizomes; excellent for dry native meadow plantings and valuable for native bees and goldfinches.
Outstanding nutrient accumulator. Young leaves cooked like spinach (cooking neutralizes sting). Rich in iron and minerals. Traditional medicine.

A native annual chenopod found in disturbed soils and open woodland edges across Alberta; leaves are edible raw or cooked as a spinach substitute and the small red strawberry-like fruits are mildly sweet and edible. An excellent pioneer species for bare or disturbed ground in Zone 3-4 permaculture systems, self-seeding readily once established. Indigenous peoples used the leaves and berries as a food source; no significant toxicity concerns but plants can accumulate oxalates so moderate consumption is advised.

A deep-rooted prairie legume native to open grasslands, dry slopes, and sandy soils in Alberta, where it fixes atmospheric nitrogen via root nodules and provides important forage for pollinators with its pale yellow flowers. The starchy taproots were consumed by Indigenous peoples, including Cree and Blackfoot, though care must be taken to distinguish it from toxic water hemlock; roots should not be eaten raw in quantity. Excellent for dryland permaculture in Zone 3-4, stabilizing poor soils and improving fertility without irrigation.

A primitive vascular plant of shallow marshes, lake margins, and slow streams across Alberta; spreads aggressively via rhizomes and should be contained in designed systems. Accumulates silica and minerals, making its biomass useful as a dynamic accumulator mulch. Not considered edible; large quantities are toxic to livestock due to thiaminase content.

NOTE: 'Sweet Broom' is ambiguous — Carmichaelia is a New Zealand genus unrelated to Alberta flora. The most likely intended species is Hedysarum alpinum (alpine sweetvetch / 'bear root' / liquorice root), a native nitrogen-fixing legume of gravelly riverbanks, open slopes, and foothills with pinnate leaves and showy one-sided racemes of pink-purple pea flowers and jointed loment pods. Roots were traditionally eaten by Indigenous peoples and bears, but WARNING: easily confused with the toxic H. boreale ssp. mackenziei (MacKenzie's Hedysarum); expert identification is essential before any consumption, and seeds contain toxic amino acids.

A sprawling woodland groundcover native to moist, shaded forest understoreys across Alberta, valued in permaculture for its ability to suppress weeds and retain soil moisture under canopy trees. The whorled leaves are fragrant when dried, containing coumarin, and were used by Indigenous peoples to scent belongings and as a mild medicinal herb; leaves can be used as a coagulant for milk in cheese-making. Non-invasive and well-suited to the shaded understory layer of Zone 3-4 food forests, pairing well with native shrubs and trees.

native_region: Northern and Western Canada, riparian and moist meadows
notes: Sweetgrass is deeply sacred to many Indigenous peoples across the prairies and boreal regions, used ceremonially for braiding and smudging; harvest should be conducted ethically and sparingly. It thrives in moist to wet soils along riparian edges and seasonally flooded meadows, helping stabilize stream banks with its rhizomatous root system. The fragrant coumarin compounds in its leaves make it distinctive; not a significant food plant but culturally irreplaceable in Alberta's Indigenous heritage.
Tall warm-season grass. Excellent for biomass and erosion control. Golden fall colour. Wildlife cover.

NOTE: 'Tall Anemone' most commonly refers to Anemone virginiana, a woodland-edge species of central and eastern North America reaching the eastern Canadian prairies; its native status at the Edmonton longitude is uncertain and it is not typically listed in core Alberta flora. Distinct from Canada Anemone (A. canadensis) already in the database (which is shorter, rhizomatous, and white-flowered). Has greenish-white to cream flowers on long stalks followed by elongated cylindrical 'thimble' seed heads releasing cottony wind-borne seeds. WARNING: all Anemone species contain protoanemonin and are toxic and skin-irritating — do not ingest.

A vigorous native goldenrod widespread across Alberta in open fields, roadsides, and disturbed prairie edges, providing one of the most important late-season nectar and pollen sources for bees and butterflies before winter. It spreads by both rhizome and seed and can form dense colonies, so siting and management are important in small permaculture gardens to prevent it from outcompeting other plantings. Young leaves and flowers are edible, and the plant has ethnobotanical use as a medicinal tea for respiratory and urinary complaints among various Indigenous nations.

Native wetland perennial of moist meadows, fens, streambanks, and cool moist boreal forest openings, with distinctive pinnately compound leaves arranged in ladder-like rows of 15–25 lance-shaped leaflets (the source of the common name) and loose clusters of nodding blue-violet bell-shaped flowers with slightly protruding stamens. Polemonium occidentale is a related western montane species — P. acutiflorum is the more widely distributed boreal/subarctic plant and the more likely Edmonton-area species. Prefers cool moist conditions and dislikes summer heat and drought.

A tall, striking perennial native to moist meadows, streambanks, and open forests in Alberta, producing impressive spikes of deep blue-purple flowers that are highly attractive to bumblebees and hummingbirds. All parts of the plant are highly toxic due to diterpenoid alkaloids, and it is one of the most significant causes of cattle poisoning on Alberta rangelands; it must be sited away from livestock and clearly labeled in any permaculture planting. Despite its toxicity it is a valuable wildlife plant and ornamental accent for wet zones in Zone 3-4 systems where children and animals are not present.

Boreal woodland perennial with nodding clusters of pink buds opening to true-blue tubular flowers, excellent for bumblebees and early pollinators. Prefers moist, humus-rich soils along streambanks, open woods, and moist meadows. Go slow on edible use — reports are limited and it should not be confused with European Mertensia species.

A tall, elegant native perennial of moist prairies, streambanks, and open floodplain forests in Alberta, prized for its delicate glaucous compound foliage and airy clusters of white to purplish flowers that support native bees and flies. The plant has modest medicinal use in Indigenous traditions, including as a dermatological and eyewash preparation, though internal use is not recommended as the plant contains alkaloids with potential toxicity. In Zone 3-4 permaculture, it is excellent for stabilizing moist or periodically flooded soil and provides a graceful vertical accent in rain gardens or woodland edge plantings.

native_region: Western Canada and northern North America
notes: Achillea alpina is a drought-tolerant native yarrow well-suited to Zone 3-4 Alberta, thriving in dry to moist open meadows, roadsides, and disturbed sites. It is a strong pollinator plant attracting beneficial insects including native bees, wasps, and butterflies, making it valuable in food forest understories and pollinator strips. Leaves and flowers have longstanding Indigenous medicinal use for wound healing, fever, and digestive ailments; can spread via rhizomes but is not considered invasive.
Only native deciduous conifer. Golden fall colour. Bark tea traditional for respiratory ailments. Tolerates wet soils.
Large maple-shaped leaves. Soft delicate fruit doesn't store well but excellent fresh. Thornless.

Thinleaf Alder is a vigorous riparian shrub or small tree that fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root symbiosis with Frankia bacteria, making it a foundational species for riparian permaculture systems in Zone 3-4 Alberta. It excels at stabilizing streambanks, filtering runoff, and providing dense wildlife cover for birds and small mammals. Its leaf litter contributes significantly to soil fertility as a dynamic accumulator, and it is an excellent pioneer species for restoring degraded wetland and riparian edges.

A distinctively triangular-stemmed emergent sedge found along lake shores and wetland margins throughout Alberta, tolerant of fluctuating water levels and moderate salinity. Rhizomes, tender shoots, and seeds are edible and historically harvested by Indigenous communities. Its dense colonies offer excellent waterfowl nesting habitat and effective shoreline stabilization for reclamation projects.

native_region: Throughout Canada and northern North America
notes: Ticklegrass is a delicate, airy native bunchgrass common across open disturbed and moist sites in Alberta, valued as a pioneer species that rapidly colonizes bare or degraded soils to prevent erosion. Its fine wispy seed heads provide late-season texture and movement in ornamental or naturalized plantings, and the seeds are consumed by small birds and ground-foraging wildlife. It is not invasive but can self-seed prolifically in disturbed conditions, making it useful for ecological restoration projects in Zone 3-4.

A robust upright rush of moist prairies, meadow depressions, and seasonally wet areas in southern and central Alberta, tolerating periodic drought once established. It provides structure and nesting material for grassland birds and insects, and its dense clumps help stabilize moist prairie soils. Seed must be obtained through specialist native plant suppliers and benefits from cold stratification before spring sowing.

Trailing Raspberry is a low-growing woodland groundcover ideal for the shaded understory of food forests and riparian woodland edges in Zone 3-4 Alberta, where it spreads by slender trailing stems to form a weed-suppressing mat. It produces small but edible red raspberries that are consumed by wildlife and humans, with historical use by Indigenous peoples of the Pacific and western mountain regions. It prefers cool, moist, humus-rich soils and is an excellent companion plant beneath taller shrubs and trees.
Most widely distributed tree in North America. Clonal colonies. Inner bark is survival food. Pioneer species.

Tufted Hairgrass is a versatile native bunchgrass thriving in moist to wet soils, wetland margins, and partially shaded sites across Zone 3-4 Alberta, where it forms dense, robust tussocks with elegant airy seed heads valued for ornamental use. It is highly effective for stabilizing wet or poorly drained soils, filtering surface runoff, and providing structure and cover in rain gardens or bioswales. The seeds are eaten by songbirds and the dense clumps provide nesting and overwintering habitat for small invertebrates.

native_region: Western Canada, riparian corridors and wetland margins
notes: Tufted Tall Manna Grass is a robust riparian grass found along stream banks, wet ditches, and pond margins in Alberta foothills and mountain regions; its dense tufts provide excellent bank stabilization. Seeds are technically edible and were historically consumed by some Indigenous groups as a minor grain source, though yields are low. It provides important cover and nesting structure for wetland birds and small mammals; well-suited to rain garden or constructed wetland edges in Zone 3-4 permaculture designs.

A robust wetland sedge of swampy thickets, fens, and floodplain edges found across Alberta's boreal and parkland zones, named for its reflexed fruiting spikes. Its dense root mass makes it highly valuable for streambank and wetland reclamation projects where soil cohesion is critical. Available primarily through reclamation seed mixes; no toxicity concerns and provides good invertebrate and bird habitat.

Twin Arnica is a drought-adapted native prairie herb of dry open grasslands and parkland edges in Alberta, producing paired bright yellow daisy-like flowers that are highly attractive to native bees and early-season pollinators. All parts of the plant contain helenalin and related sesquiterpene lactones making it toxic if ingested; it is used externally only in traditional and modern herbal medicine for bruising, muscle soreness, and inflammation. It spreads slowly by rhizome to form small colonies and is well suited to dry prairie or meadow permaculture plantings in Zone 3-4.
Delicate paired pink bells. Trailing evergreen groundcover. Named by Linnaeus as his favourite plant.

Native climbing/twining woody vine of aspen parkland edges and open mixed woods, with pairs of oval leaves fused at the tips just below the flowers forming a distinctive cup, topped by clusters of yellow-to-reddish-orange tubular flowers followed by clusters of bright translucent red-orange berries. Excellent for hummingbirds and a valuable small native trellis plant (to ~2–3 m), much less aggressive than introduced honeysuckles. WARNING: berries are not considered reliably edible — do not eat. Unlike the invasive Asian L. japonica, this native twiner is well-behaved.

A low-growing cushion forb native to dry, rocky slopes and gravelly soils of the eastern Rocky Mountain foothills in Alberta; it is an exceptional pollinator plant attracting native bees, butterflies, and beetles with its sulphur-yellow umbellate flower clusters. Seeds were used as a food source by some Indigenous peoples of the western interior. Non-invasive and highly suited to xeriscape and rock garden permaculture designs in Zone 3-4.

FLAG: Valeriana officinalis is the commonly sold medicinal valerian and is NON-NATIVE to Alberta; the true Alberta native is Valeriana sitchensis (Sitka valerian), found in moist montane and subalpine habitats along the eastern slopes. V. sitchensis shares sedative and anxiolytic root compounds with V. officinalis and has Indigenous medicinal application. V. officinalis can naturalize aggressively in disturbed moist areas and should not be planted in or near natural riparian zones in Alberta.

A slender, evergreen, rush-like horsetail of wetland margins, stream gravels, and calcareous fens; one of Alberta's smaller horsetails and far less aggressive than Equisetum arvense or E. hyemale, making it more suitable for managed wetland and bioswale permaculture edges. FLAG: All horsetails contain thiaminase and are toxic to livestock if consumed in quantity; not recommended as forage and should be noted in grazing contexts. Its silica-rich biomass contributes as a dynamic accumulator and stabilizes saturated or seasonally flooded soils.

Native woodland-edge perennial of moist aspen parkland, meadows, and partially shaded slopes, with delicate blue-green compound leaves divided into many small three-lobed leaflets bearing prominent veining on the undersides, and airy panicles of small apetalous flowers with dangling cream-yellow stamens (male plants showiest). Dioecious and wind-pollinated rather than insect-pollinated. Foliage resembles columbine or maidenhair fern and provides excellent fine texture in shade plantings. Spreads modestly by rhizome in cool moist humusy soils.
Wild blueberry. Requires acidic soil. Low spreading shrub. Sweet berries.

Native emergent aquatic perennial of shallow pond margins, sphagnum bogs, and slow boreal streams, with creeping rhizomes rooted in mud or floating peat, glossy heart-shaped leaves, and showy white spathes (resembling miniature calla lilies) surrounding a short yellow spadix, followed by clusters of bright red berries. WARNING: all parts contain calcium oxalate crystals and are acutely toxic and mouth-burning when raw; historical use as a famine starch required extensive cooking/drying to destroy the oxalates. Treat as ornamental only — do not eat. Beautiful and reliable in permanently wet bog gardens or pond edges.
Small multi-stemmed birch of stream banks. Sap drinkable. Reddish-brown bark. Good riparian restoration tree.

Riparian and aquatic margin specialist native to Alberta sloughs and stream edges; provides important late-summer nectar for native pollinators. CAUTION: Closely resembles the extremely toxic Water Hemlock (Cicuta maculata); misidentification is potentially fatal and no edible use should be attempted without expert verification. Best used in permaculture solely for ecological function: bank stabilization, pollinator support, and wildlife habitat.

One of the most widespread and cold-hardy sedges in Alberta, thriving in marshes, lake margins, fens, and bioswales from the prairies through to the subarctic. Its vigorous rhizomatous spread and tolerance of prolonged inundation make it a first-choice species for bioswale construction and wetland reclamation. Commercially available through K&S Growers; provides essential forage for muskrats and nesting habitat for numerous wetland bird species.

Native amphibious perennial (formerly Polygonum amphibium) occurring in two dramatically different growth forms: an aquatic form with floating lance-shaped leaves and emergent spikes of bright pink flowers rising from rhizomes rooted in pond bottoms, and a terrestrial form with erect hairy stems and narrower leaves on muddy shorelines and ditches. The two forms can interconvert depending on water level. Excellent shoreline stabilizer, important waterfowl food (seeds), and supports many native bees and butterflies. Spreads aggressively by rhizome in shallow water.

FLAG: All parts of Iris missouriensis are toxic if ingested, particularly the rhizome and seeds, causing severe gastrointestinal distress; it should not be planted where children or livestock have unsupervised access. This native prairie iris colonizes moist meadows, seasonally wet depressions, and streamside grasslands across southwestern Alberta, forming dense clumps that stabilize soils and provide early-season pollinator habitat for native bees and hummingbirds. Some Indigenous groups historically used the fibrous leaves for cordage, though any medicinal or internal use carries significant toxicity risk.
White violet with yellow centre. Shade tolerant woodland species. Edible flowers.

A slender, shade-tolerant bunchgrass of open coniferous forests and rocky eastern slope habitats in Alberta, often found under lodgepole pine and along forest edges; it is a specialist species with limited commercial availability and is less commonly used in restoration than its relative Festuca campestris. Western Fescue provides fine-textured, low-biomass groundcover that reduces soil erosion on shaded slopes and supports seed-eating birds and small mammals. It is non-aggressive and does not spread invasively, making it ideal for understory polyculture plantings in food forest designs.

A late-blooming prairie aster of dry to mesic native grasslands across Alberta, providing one of the most critical late-season nectar and pollen sources for migrating monarchs, native bumblebees, and specialist Andrena and Lasioglossum bees before winter.
