Symphyotrichum campestre spreads slowly by rhizome to form naturalistic clumps and is well-suited to prairie garden edges, pollinator corridors, and dryland food forest understories. It is not invasive and its late bloom window fills an important phenological gap in Zone 3-4 permaculture plantings.

A dioecious woodland herb of moist montane and subalpine forests, often found along stream edges and in shaded meadows. Some Indigenous peoples used root preparations medicinally, though all parts should be considered potentially toxic and are not recommended for consumption. Provides excellent habitat structure and nectar resources in shaded food forest understory layers.

Native multi-stemmed large shrub or small tree of moist foothills and mountain slopes, with pinnately compound leaves of 9–13 sharply toothed leaflets, flat-topped clusters of small white flowers, and striking clusters of bright orange-red pomes ripening in late summer and persisting into winter. Fruits are astringent-bitter raw but become palatable after frost and are excellent for jellies, syrups, and wildlife forage — critical late-season food for bohemian waxwings, pine grosbeaks, and bears. Sorbus sitchensis is a close relative with fewer, broader leaflets and more Pacific distribution; S. scopulina is the standard Alberta native.

A dominant bunchgrass of dry mixed-grass and fescue prairie in Alberta, valued for its striking awned seed heads that aid wind dispersal and create dramatic winter ornamental interest. Its deep fibrous roots make it highly drought-tolerant and effective for erosion control on exposed slopes and dry embankments. Seeds were historically gathered by some Plains Indigenous peoples; sharp awns can penetrate livestock skin and should be managed accordingly in grazing systems.
Dense thicket-forming shrub. White berries persist into winter. Medicinal use external only — berries mildly toxic internally. Good for erosion.

A rhizomatous sod-forming grass and keystone species of Alberta's native prairie, spreading aggressively by underground rhizomes to form dense erosion-resistant mats ideal for disturbed site restoration. It is highly tolerant of alkaline soils, periodic drought, and cold winters, making it a reliable pioneer species for degraded dry prairie and roadsides in Zone 3-4. Seeds were used as an emergency food source by some Indigenous peoples, and the species provides critical early-season forage and overwintering cover for native wildlife.

Native wetland/moist-meadow aster (sometimes treated as a western segregate of Symphyotrichum lanceolatum) of streambanks, ditches, fen margins, and moist prairie, with narrow willow-like lance-shaped leaves and open panicles of small pale blue to nearly white ray flowers around yellow disks. Spreads by rhizomes to form loose colonies; excellent late-summer nectar source for migrating butterflies and native bees. Taxonomically unsettled — some treatments lump it with S. lanceolatum or S. subspicatum, so flag if the user prefers a broader species concept.
Spectacular orange-red upfacing lily. Increasingly rare — never harvest from wild. Buy nursery-propagated.

TOXIC - ALL PARTS: White Camas contains steroidal alkaloids including zygacine and is highly toxic to humans, livestock, and pets; it must be clearly flagged in any permaculture database and planted away from edible bulb species. It closely resembles edible blue camas (Camassia) and wild onion (Allium) species and has caused fatal poisonings; never harvest or consume any part of this plant. Despite its toxicity it provides valuable pollinator habitat in moist alpine and subalpine meadows and is an ecologically important native wildflower for Zone 3-4 ornamental restoration plantings.

A fragrant white-flowered evening primrose of dry sandy prairie and open grasslands, with blooms that open at dusk to attract night-flying moth pollinators including hawkmoths, making it a key species for nocturnal pollinator support in Alberta permaculture. Roots, young shoots, and seeds are edible and were used as food and medicine by Plains Indigenous peoples; seeds are rich in gamma-linolenic acid similar to cultivated evening primrose oil. It spreads by rhizomes and can colonize disturbed sandy soils, providing effective erosion control without becoming aggressively invasive in native plant communities.
White flowers with pink veins. Woodland edge and meadow. Long bloom period.

A mat-forming evergreen subshrub and critical nitrogen-fixing pioneer of alpine and subalpine rocky slopes in Alberta, forming symbiotic associations with Frankia actinomycetes to fix atmospheric nitrogen and build soil fertility on disturbed or glacially scoured substrates. Its feathery seed plumes aid wind dispersal and the dense mats stabilize talus, gravelly moraines, and alpine meadow edges against erosion, making it invaluable for high-elevation restoration. Dryas is a keystone species in post-glacial succession and its dried leaves were historically used for tea by some Indigenous and northern peoples, though this is not a primary recommended use.
Nitrogen-fixing legume. White cylindrical flower spikes. Deep taproot makes it very drought tolerant.
Needle tea rich in vitamin C. Pitch used as wound sealant and chewing gum. Dominant boreal tree.

A specialist dry woodland grass adapted to dappled shade under boreal and mixedwood forest canopies in Zone 3-4 Alberta. Seeds were historically harvested and consumed by some Indigenous peoples as a grain-like food source. Valuable for understory groundcover and soil stabilization in shaded permaculture food forest systems; non-invasive and slow to establish.
Bee balm. One of the best native pollinator plants. Earl Grey-like tea. Traditional cold/flu remedy. Drought tolerant.

Distinct from other native currants by its strongly aromatic black fruit and resin-dotted leaves; widely available from Alberta native plant nurseries and well-suited to Zone 3-4 food forest understories and riparian edges. Berries are edible raw or cooked and were an important food source for many Indigenous Plains and Woodland peoples; leaves have documented medicinal uses. An alternate host for white pine blister rust (Cronartium ribicola), so avoid planting near five-needled pines.
Sky-blue flowers open daily and drop. Fibre plant historically. Excellent in dry meadow plantings.

IMPORTANT: Nepeta cataria is NOT native to Alberta or North America — it is a Eurasian introduction now widely naturalized on roadsides, farmyards, and disturbed ground across Canada. Aromatic mint-family perennial with grey-green softly hairy heart-shaped leaves and terminal spikes of small white flowers spotted with purple. Famous for producing euphoria in cats (via the volatile iridoid nepetalactone) and used as a mild sedative herbal tea for humans. The essential oil is a potent mosquito repellent. Self-seeds freely and can naturalize; plant thoughtfully.

Native variety is var. sibiricum (not the European var. schoenoprasum). Ensure nursery stock is native provenance. Native form of common chives. Purple globe flowers excellent pollinator. All parts edible.

BORDERLINE FOR EDMONTON: More reliably found in southern Alberta river valleys. Zone 5a+ typically; may struggle in Edmonton's zone 3b-4a. Vigorous native vine. White flower clusters. Fluffy seedheads. Only native clematis on prairies.
Thorny shrub. Fruit excellent for preserves and pies. Tolerates shade well.
Legume — fixes nitrogen. Root has sweet licorice flavour. Traditional sore throat remedy. Bur-like seed pods.
Tiny white flower clusters. Woodland groundcover. Red berries in late summer (mildly toxic). Spreads by rhizome.
Only mint species native to North America. Spreads aggressively. Tea and culinary. Natural pest repellent.
Leaf tea is traditional for women's health. Spreads vigorously by suckers. Fruit identical to cultivated raspberry.
Trailing currant of moist forests. Tart fruit good for jelly and sauce.
Woodland plant. Root tea traditional tonic. Shade tolerant. Blue-black berries for wildlife (not palatable to humans).
Excellent edible groundcover. Spreads by runners. Intense flavour. Leaf tea is traditional.
Climbing/trailing legume. Purple-blue flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Good for mixed plantings.

One of Alberta's tallest native asters, Willow Aster thrives in moist to wet-mesic areas such as stream banks, ditches, and meadow edges, making it an excellent Zone 3-4 permaculture plant for water retention zones and riparian buffers. Its late-season bloom is critically important for migrating and overwintering pollinators, particularly native bees and butterflies, providing nectar well into autumn. Can spread aggressively by rhizome in rich, moist soils; manage spread in smaller garden settings.

Winterfat is a highly drought and alkaline-tolerant native shrub of Alberta's dry prairies and plains, making it one of the few woody options for challenging saline or calcareous soils in Zone 3-4 permaculture systems. Its woolly white fruiting plumes provide exceptional winter wildlife forage, particularly for deer, pronghorn, and sage grouse, and the plant retains nutritious biomass through the coldest months. Available through ALCLA Native Plant Nursery; non-invasive and well-behaved in dry landscape designs.

A highly adaptable and rhizomatous rush native across Alberta, ideal for bioswales, rain gardens, and wet meadow margins due to its tolerance of fluctuating moisture, compacted soils, and mild salinity. Its wiry stems and creeping rhizomes bind soils effectively and support invertebrate communities. Widely available from K&S Growers and other Alberta native plant nurseries, making it one of the most accessible native rushes for permaculture water management.

NOTE: 'Wood Betony' in Alberta most commonly refers to Pedicularis bracteosa (bracted lousewort); P. canadensis is an eastern species not native to Alberta. Native hemiparasitic perennial of moist foothills and boreal forest openings, with fern-like pinnate leaves and dense terminal spikes of hooded yellow (sometimes reddish) tubular flowers subtended by leafy bracts. IMPORTANT: like all Pedicularis, it is a root hemiparasite requiring specific host plants (often grasses, sedges, or conifer roots) to establish — VERY difficult to cultivate from seed or transplant, and essentially never available from nurseries. Admire in the wild rather than attempting to garden.
Alpine strawberry type. Smaller fruit than F. virginiana but more aromatic. Shade tolerant groundcover.

Alberta's most common native rose and a cornerstone shrub for Zone 3-4 permaculture hedgerows, food forests, and wildlife corridors; widely stocked by ALCLA and other native nurseries. Rose hips are exceptionally high in vitamin C and were a critical winter food and medicine for many First Nations peoples; petals are also edible and aromatic. Spreads by rhizome and can form dense thickets that provide excellent erosion control and wildlife shelter; manage spread in smaller installations.

Woolly Cinquefoil is a drought-adapted native of Alberta's dry prairies and foothill grasslands, recognizable by its densely white-woolly silvery foliage and bright yellow flowers; an excellent ornamental groundcover for Zone 3-4 xeric permaculture designs. Its deep taproot improves soil structure and accesses subsoil nutrients, functioning as a dynamic accumulator in compacted or degraded prairie soils. Non-invasive and well-suited to alkaline or gravelly soils where few other forbs thrive; available through WAF and similar native plant programs.

native_region: Across Canada and northern United States
notes: A densely tufted wetland sedge of marshes, wet meadows, and disturbed riparian areas, identifiable by its woolly-hairy perigynia and broad leaves. Well suited for inclusion in native seed mixes targeting wet disturbed sites and roadside bioswales in Alberta's parkland and boreal transition zones. Occasionally available through native seed mixes; no known toxicity, and its persistent structure provides overwintering cover for small mammals and ground-nesting birds.
Traditional wound herb (styptic). Anti-inflammatory tea. Accumulates potassium, phosphorus, copper. Top pollinator.

A tall umbelliferous herb of moist montane meadows and woodland edges in Alberta; provides excellent pollinator habitat with its flat-topped flower clusters. Roots and seeds have been used medicinally by Indigenous peoples, but this plant contains furanocoumarins and should not be confused with toxic relatives; handle with care to avoid phototoxic sap. Monocarpic in behaviour, often dying after seeding; allow self-seeding for continuity in a food forest understory.

Widespread native rose-family perennial of moist meadows, aspen parkland, ditches, and forest edges, with pinnately divided basal leaves and branched stems bearing small bright yellow five-petaled flowers followed by bur-like fruiting heads with hooked styles that cling to clothing and animal fur. One of the most common Geum species across Alberta. Closely resembles Large-leaved Avens (G. macrophyllum, Batch 2) and the two are often confused — G. aleppicum has smaller terminal leaflets relative to the laterals. Roots are mildly astringent and have a weak clove scent.

One of Alberta's few yellow-flowered penstemons, native to moist meadows, open slopes, and riparian margins in the foothills and parkland zones. It is a valuable early-season pollinator plant, especially for native bumblebees and small specialist bees adapted to tubular flowers. Low-maintenance and long-lived in well-drained but moisture-retaining soils; excellent for naturalizing edges of food forest glades.

native_region: Western Canada, Great Plains, Rocky Mountain foothills
notes: A mat-forming xeric herb of dry rocky slopes, gravelly prairie, and exposed ridges across Alberta; extremely drought-tolerant and valuable for stabilizing disturbed dry soils. Its sulfur-yellow flower clusters are a critical nectar source for native bees, butterflies, and pollinating beetles in arid landscapes. Deep taproots mine subsoil minerals making it a useful dynamic accumulator; never transplant from the wild as taproots are very sensitive to disturbance.

Native Rocky Mountain columbine of subalpine meadows, open montane forests, and foothills seepages, with delicate ternately compound blue-green foliage and nodding pale yellow flowers featuring five curved spurs holding nectar at their tips — a classic hummingbird and long-tongued bumblebee plant. Distinct from the blue-flowered Blue Columbine (Aquilegia brevistyla) already in the database. WARNING: all parts (especially seeds) contain cardiogenic glycosides and cyanogenic compounds and should not be ingested. Naturally occurs in Alberta's western mountains and foothills; hardy in Edmonton gardens in cool moist partial shade.

native_region: Great Plains, Prairie Provinces of Canada
notes: A slender annual of dry mixed-grass and fescue prairie, ideally suited as a pioneer and gap-filler in sandy or gravelly disturbed soils across Alberta's dry zones. Like other flax species, seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides and should not be consumed in quantity; traditional use was limited. Direct-sow in early spring into open disturbed ground; self-seeds reliably and integrates well into dry prairie polycultures or the sunny edge of a food forest.

Alberta's most iconic native orchid, occurring in moist boreal woodlands, fens, and calcareous riparian areas; protected under provincial legislation and must never be collected from the wild. It requires a specific mycorrhizal fungal partner for germination and establishment, making transplanting from nursery stock extremely challenging and often unsuccessful without inoculated substrate. Medicinally used as a nervine by several Indigenous nations but considered at-risk; best role in permaculture is as an indicator of mature, undisturbed woodland soil ecology.

native_region: Western North America, Pacific Coast to Rockies
edible_parts: young leaves and stems (mild, raw or cooked)
notes: A bright-flowered riparian annual or short-lived perennial of streambanks, seeps, and wet meadow margins across Alberta's foothills and mountains; behaves as an annual in Zone 3-4 but self-seeds prolifically in moist ground. Young foliage is edible and was consumed by some Indigenous peoples; it is non-toxic and mild-flavoured. Excellent for stabilizing moist riparian margins in permaculture designs, filtering runoff, and supporting bumble bees and hummingbirds as a late-succession wet-edge pollinator plant.

Dryas drummondii is a pioneering nitrogen-fixing groundcover that colonizes glacial outwash, gravelly riverbanks, and rocky alpine slopes across Alberta, forming dense mats that stabilize disturbed soils. Its root-associated actinobacteria (Frankia) fix atmospheric nitrogen, making it a critical early successional species for building soil fertility in harsh Zone 3-4 environments. The feathery, twisted seed plumes are distinctive and it is not known to be toxic or invasive.

Orthocarpus luteus is a hemiparasitic annual that attaches to the roots of host grasses and forbs via haustoria, drawing water and nutrients while still photosynthesizing; it is a specialist species unsuitable for conventional cultivation without appropriate host plants present. It inhabits dry, open prairies and disturbed grasslands across Alberta and is an important late-season nectar source for specialist native bees. Not known to be toxic or invasive; its hemiparasitic nature should be flagged in any planting design database to prevent inadvertent host plant damage.

HEMIPARASITIC: Castilleja lutescens is a root hemiparasite that attaches to neighbouring grasses and forbs via haustoria and cannot be reliably established without suitable host plants; flag in database and do not design as a standalone planting. It occupies dry prairie and foothill grasslands in Alberta and is a valuable nectar source for hummingbirds and long-tongued bees. Flowers were reportedly consumed in small quantities by some Indigenous peoples, but selenium bioaccumulation from host plants in seleniferous soils poses a toxicity risk and ingestion is not recommended.

Floating-leaf aquatic native to Alberta lakes and ponds; large rhizomes anchor in muddy substrates and leaves shade water to reduce algal growth in permaculture ponds. Seeds and rhizomes were used as food by multiple Indigenous nations including Cree and Ojibwe; seeds can be parched and ground into flour and rhizomes roasted, though preparation is required to reduce bitterness. Provides important shelter and food for waterfowl, turtles, and aquatic insects.

Lithospermum ruderale is a deep-rooted, drought-tolerant prairie forb that is one of Alberta's earliest spring bloomers, providing critical early nectar for native bees and queen bumblebees emerging from overwintering. The waxy white nutlets and deep taproot contain lithospermic acid and alkaloids historically used by some Indigenous peoples as a contraceptive preparation; internal use should be avoided without expert guidance due to potential hormonal effects and the plant should be flagged for medicinal toxicity. The red-purple dye yielded by the roots was also used traditionally for body paint and decoration.

Native annual hemiparasite of grassy meadows, roadsides, and fens, with opposite lance-shaped toothed leaves and bright yellow tubular flowers emerging from inflated green calyces that turn brown and papery in fruit — ripe seeds rattle audibly inside the capsule (the source of the common name). CRITICAL MEADOW MANAGEMENT TOOL: parasitizes grass roots and significantly reduces grass vigour, opening space for wildflowers to establish; widely used in native meadow/'prairie restoration' seeding mixes to weaken dominant grasses. Must be direct-sown in autumn (requires cold stratification) and self-seeds readily once established. WARNING: seeds contain toxic glycosides and should not be eaten.

Native nitrogen-fixing legume of dry gravelly prairies, open foothills slopes, and disturbed calcareous ground, with silvery-silky pinnate leaves in a basal tuft and short leafless stalks bearing dense one-sided racemes of pale cream-yellow pea flowers (the closely related O. monticola is very similar and taxonomically sometimes lumped together). WARNING: contains the alkaloid swainsonine and causes 'locoism' — a serious chronic neurological poisoning syndrome — in horses, cattle, and sheep that graze it repeatedly; do not plant where livestock will access. The tips of the keel petals end in a distinctive sharp point, distinguishing Oxytropis from the similar Astragalus.

native_region: Western Canada, Columbia Basin, dry rocky slopes
notes: Fritillaria pudica is a small spring-ephemeral geophyte of dry, rocky grasslands and sagebrush slopes at the western edge of Alberta's range, emerging very early and completing its above-ground life cycle before summer drought; bulbs must be planted in sharply drained soils to prevent rot. The starchy bulbs and small rice-grain bulblets were an important food source for many Interior and Plains Indigenous peoples, eaten raw, steamed, or dried, though care must be taken to avoid confusion with toxic species such as death camas (Anticlea elegans) which occupies similar habitats. Not invasive; populations are locally at risk from grazing and collection pressure, so harvest from wild stands is strongly discouraged.

Draba incerta is a tiny cushion-forming crucifer of high-elevation rocky outcrops, scree slopes, and alpine meadows in the Alberta Rockies, and one of the earliest flowering plants to bloom after snowmelt, providing a critical early pollen resource for emerging native bees and flies. Its compact rosette form and tolerance of skeletal, nutrient-poor soils make it an excellent pioneer for rock garden and alpine permaculture contexts in Zone 3-4. Not toxic or invasive; it is relatively uncommon and best established from nursery-grown stock rather than wild collection.
